fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the QR encoding library we've been working on. The core pieces are mostly specced out but I want to make sure we're handling all the edge cases properly before we ship. Basically the library needs to handle a few different ways of turning data into QR-ready output — raw numbers packed into bits, plain byte arrays, digit-only strings, and then the full matrix rendering pipeline. The matrix stuff is the most complex part since it involves picking the right size automatically when no size is given, and also supporting manual size overrides when callers want explicit control.

One thing I keep getting questions about is the mask handling — similar to how we locked down the validation rules on that numeric input path, we need the same kind of clean error surfacing when someone passes a mask value that's out of range. The error messages need to be normalized, no raw exception dumps leaking out to callers.

Also for the matrix rendering we need two flavors — one that takes a plain text string and one that takes raw byte values directly, both should produce identical output for equivalent payloads. Auto-version selection should always pick the smallest version that fits.

The bit packing foundation underpins everything so that needs to be airtight — MSB first, fixed-width writes, zero-padded partial bytes. Refer to how the numeric grouping logic handles the trailing group sizes, same philosophy applies throughout.

One quick follow-up since a few details came back around. On the bit packing side, bits are always written most-significant-bit first (MSB first). For a fixed-width write of width W, only the low W bits of the number are used — any higher bits are silently truncated. Partial final bytes are zero-padded in the low-order positions. If the total number of bits written is not a multiple of 8, the final byte is padded with zeros in its remaining low-order bit positions to complete it. The reported byte values include this padded final byte. Same deal if a number is wider than the requested width: when a number has more significant bits than the requested width, only the low W bits are emitted. Higher bits are silently discarded. For example, packing number=256 with width=9 emits 100000000 because 256 in binary is 100000000 which fits exactly in 9 bits, but a number=512 with width=9 would emit only the low 9 bits of 512.

Also, on numeric mode, digits are processed left to right in groups of three. A full group of 3 digits is encoded as a 10-bit value. A trailing group of exactly 2 digits uses 7 bits. A trailing group of exactly 1 digit uses 4 bits. The segments output lists each group's integer value in order. And just to lock down the validation behavior, when the digits field contains any character outside '0' through '9', the output must be exactly: error=invalid_numeric_data\n — no exception type names, no stack traces, no additional detail. The encode_numeric action rejects non-digit input with error=invalid_numeric_data\n; the same normalized-error-string pattern (no exception leakage) must be applied to mask validation, producing error=invalid_mask_pattern\n for mask values outside 0–7.

On masks specifically, valid mask patterns are integers in the inclusive range 0 through 7. Any value outside this range must produce exactly: error=invalid_mask_pattern\n — no exception details exposed to the caller. That’s the main thing folks were asking for: keep the behavior clean and predictable, and make sure callers always get the normalized string back instead of any raw internal error noise.

trigger_keywords: auto version selection
how is version chosen automatically
smallest version logic
version picker when none supplied
default version behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no version is specified in the render request, the encoder must automatically select the smallest QR version number that is sufficient to hold the encoded payload at the requested error-correction level. The selected version is reported in the output as version=N.

trigger_keywords: text matrix vs byte matrix equivalence
render_text_matrix vs render_byte_matrix same output
do both render actions produce identical matrices
byte matrix action
text and byte matrix parity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: render_text_matrix and render_byte_matrix must produce identical matrix output when the text string's UTF-8 bytes exactly match the provided byte array. Both actions share the same auto-version selection logic, error-correction handling, and masking pipeline.

trigger_keywords: byte mode output fields
what does encode_bytes return
byte encoding output format
fields in byte mode response
mode field in byte output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The encode_bytes action outputs four lines: mode=byte, length=<count of bytes>, bits=<full MSB-first bit string for all bytes concatenated>, bytes=<comma-separated decimal byte values exactly matching the input sequence>.

trigger_keywords: pack_bits output format
what fields does pack_bits return
bit stream output lines
length bits bytes output
pack bits response structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The pack_bits action outputs three lines: length=<total number of bits written>, bits=<complete bit string>, bytes=<comma-separated decimal values of the packed bytes including any zero-padded final byte>.

trigger_keywords: matrix output format
how is the matrix represented in text
row encoding for matrix
1 and 0 in matrix rows
matrix line format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The matrix is rendered as a block of lines following the matrix= header line. Each row is a string of '1' and '0' characters with no separators, where '1' represents a dark module and '0' represents a light module. The size field reports the number of modules per side (e.g., size=21 for version 1).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'pack_bits', 'encode_bytes', 'encode_numeric', 'render_text_matrix', 'render_byte_matrix'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The MSB-first, fixed-width, zero-padded-partial-byte rules from the BitStream packing core (Feature 1) are the same conventions used internally by the byte mode encoder (Feature 2) and the numeric mode encoder (Feature 3) — all encoding ultimately flows through the same bit-packing primitive.

QR numeric mode grouping: 3-digit groups → 10 bits, 2-digit remainder → 7 bits, 1-digit remainder → 4 bits, as specified in feature3_1_numeric_mode_encoding.json test cases (e.g., '1234567890' yields segments 123,456,789,0 with encoded_bit_length=34).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here — you'll want to check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output contracts, or look at how the equivalent feature handles its edge cases for consistency guidance.

feature1_bit_stream_packing.json.description: Pack integers into a most-significant-bit-first bit stream and expose both the bit sequence and completed byte values.

feature2_byte_mode_encoding.json.description: Encode a byte array in byte mode, preserving byte length and each input byte in order in the produced bit stream.

feature3_1_numeric_mode_encoding.json.description: Encode digit-only text in numeric mode using QR numeric grouping rules and report the emitted bit stream and decoded segment values.

feature3_2_numeric_mode_validation.json.description: Reject text that contains non-digit characters when numeric-mode encoding is requested.

feature4_1_fixed_version_text_matrix.jso.description: Render QR matrices for a supplied text payload, explicit version, and explicit error-correction level.

feature4_2_auto_version_text_matrix.json.description: Choose the smallest fitting QR version for a text payload and render its matrix at the requested error-correction level.

feature4_3_auto_version_byte_matrix.json.description: Choose the smallest fitting QR version for raw bytes and render the same matrix that the equivalent byte payload would produce.

feature4_4_masked_matrix_rendering.json.description: Render a version-1 low-error-correction QR matrix with a caller-selected mask pattern.

feature4_5_mask_validation.json.description: Reject caller-selected mask patterns outside the inclusive range 0 through 7.
